pemPublicPlaceholder: Paste PEM public key (SPKI: -----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----)
pemPrivatePlaceholder: Paste PEM private key (PKCS8: -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----)
pemHint: Only PKCS8 / SPKI accepted. Convert with: openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -nocrypt -in old.pem -out pkcs8.pem

errors.invalidPemFormat: Unrecognized PEM format. Expected -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY----- (PKCS8) or -----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY----- (SPKI).
errors.pkcs1NotSupported: PKCS1 keys are not supported. Convert with: openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -nocrypt -in pkcs1.pem -out pkcs8.pem  (private)  /  openssl rsa -in pkcs1.pem -RSAPublicKey_in -pubout -out spki.pem  (public)
errors.sec1NotSupported: SEC1 EC keys are not supported. Convert with: openssl pkcs8 -topk8 -nocrypt -in sec1.pem -out pkcs8.pem
errors.encryptedKeyNotSupported: Encrypted PEM keys are not supported. Decrypt first with: openssl pkcs8 -in encrypted.pem -out plain.pem
errors.certNotSupported: Paste a public key, not a certificate. Extract with: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -pubkey -noout
errors.expectedPublic: Verify needs a public key (-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----), not a private key
errors.expectedPrivate: Sign needs a private key (-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----), not a public key
errors.algNoneRejected: alg=none is rejected — this token is not signed and verification is not meaningful

descriptions.aeoDefinition: JWT Debugger is a free online tool for encoding, decoding, and verifying JSON Web Tokens. Supports HS256, RS256, ES256 and more algorithms. All processing runs in your browser — no tokens are sent to any server.
descriptions.whatIs: JSON Web Token (JWT) is an open standard (RFC 7519) for securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object. JWTs are commonly used for authentication and authorization in web applications. Debug tokens here, then verify payloads with [Base64](/base64) decoding or check key integrity with [Hashing](/hashing).
descriptions.useCasesP1: Authenticating API requests — include a JWT in the Authorization header (Bearer token) to verify the caller's identity and permissions.
descriptions.useCasesP2: Decoding and debugging tokens during development — inspect the header and payload claims (iss, sub, exp, iat) without writing code.
descriptions.useCasesP3: Verifying token signatures against a shared secret (HMAC) or public key (RSA/ECDSA) to ensure integrity before trusting the claims.
descriptions.step1Desc: Copy the full JWT string (three Base64URL segments separated by dots) and paste it into the Decode tab input field.
descriptions.step2Desc: The tool automatically decodes the header (algorithm, type) and payload (claims) into readable JSON. Check the exp claim to see if the token has expired.
descriptions.step3Desc: Switch to the Verify section, enter the secret or public key, and click Verify to confirm the token was signed by the expected party.
descriptions.faq1Q: Are JWTs secure for sensitive data?
descriptions.faq1A: JWT payloads are Base64Url-encoded, not encrypted — anyone can read them. Never store sensitive data in a JWT unless you use JWE (JSON Web Encryption).
descriptions.faq2Q: What's the difference between HMAC and RSA JWT algorithms?
descriptions.faq2A: HMAC algorithms (HS256/384/512) use a shared secret — both the issuer and verifier know the same key. RSA algorithms (RS256/384/512) use a public/private key pair — the issuer signs with the private key, and anyone with the public key can verify. Use HMAC for server-to-server, RSA for distributed systems where the verifier shouldn't be able to create tokens.
descriptions.faq3Q: What happens when a JWT expires?
descriptions.faq3A: The exp claim in the payload specifies the expiration timestamp. After that time, the token should be rejected by the server. This tool shows you the decoded exp so you can check whether a token is still valid. Note that JWT expiration is a claim, not enforced by the token itself — the server must validate it.
